pages.text:
double double glazing & 18ins insulation in the roof only has one radiator. I enjoyed looking at the organic gardening. "Our earth is not a gift from our parents, it is on loan from our children."

     We had a delicious filthy veggie lunch, then came home via __Woodpecker Hall__, Monmouth, but the Goodiers weren't there, only an Australia fly-swat.

__July 27th Monday__ S.E.W.A.J.C. (Thomas Train Crew)
     We left home after Tom's visit at 9.40. & headed north. We stopped for lunch in a field next to such a smelly pig-muck spreader that Charles & William were fed up & we moved into the village
     Heavy traffic on the roads, so we reached __North Shields__, __New Castle__, at 4.0., just in time for the queue to get on the Ferry to Bergen.

__July 28th Tuesday__
     The ferry was extraordinarily calm. We left at 3.30, but the ramp hit the bottom

of the van, & ripped off the water tank bracket. William & Eve went back to find the missing wooden board. We parked in a side street & Simon almost mended it. We looked around Bryggen - the old Hanseatic league wharves.
	We arrived at the campsite 10 miles out of __Bergen__, __Breistein__, * * * ½ at 6.15. There are little one room cabins to rent - a long bunk to sleep 4 people, cupboard, tables, chairs, shelves, no plumbing. There are bigger houses to rent - 2 bed/sitting rooms, kitchen, bathroom. The showers operate from a central switch board.
	The boys played football. Charles found a sand pit.
	It is amazingly light late & early - even the dark isn't very dark.

__Wednesday__
	We woke early, a shower of rain then warm sun. It rained intermittently all day, large, widely-spaced drops. Not an English drizzle. Mars Bars £1.50 (30p at home)
	As we got into the centre of Bergen, the van stopped & wouldn't go. Eve &

the boys went off to sightsee while Simon waited for the mechanic. He was only about 18, & soon saw a loose electrical connection.
	Eve & the boys ate delicious smoked salmon & shrimp french rolls, in the market, with a green sauce, & then danish pastries. [?John bought] a reindeer skin. Then on to the Bryggen Museum which shows Medieval archeological finds in the wharves. It is on a par with Stonehenge. It is a fabulous museum. The German Hanseatic league had an office there. We saw a shoe & a cow tether, fireplace tiles, gold ring....
	We saw St. Mary's church & then met up with Simon at the Hanseatic Museum. It was a very hard life. Boys of 14 were apprenticed for 6 years. They slept 2 to a bed in cupboards, tough initiation rites. They got cod fish, dried in the Lofoten Islands, from the north of Norway & brought grain from Germany.
	At about 5.30 we set off up

the funicular for a marvellous view of Bergen + the surrounding water. We had icecreams, then went down to the van + back to the campsite.

__Thursday.__
	There was hot sunshine when we woke up this morning. We set out for

Voss, through road tunnels, some of them badly ventilated.
	The scenery is beautiful. Deep fiords + lakes, snow on the mountains. Rows of fence posts + wires over which the farmers have hung hay to dry. Alternatively the hay is bagged in white bags as silage. Much evidence of cattle farming, but we have only seen two cows.
	The scenery is quite American with weatherboard houses dotted about the valleys. Barns. The bus stops have grass growing out of the roofs.
	We have stopped by a lake for lunch. William reckons the lake is polluted. He + Peter have been trying to fish with his rod. Charles changed into his swimsuit, but didn't swim.
	After a series of tunnels we went over a huge mountain pass. As usual, we were low on petrol.... + as usual Simon said, "I meant to get some before...."

captions: CHRISTIANIA S732 H + D. LADIES' SIZES: S - XL
pages.text:
On Thursday morning we stopped at __Oslo__ to buy knitwear. Peter bought a hat, Eve bought a cardigan "Christiania" is the old name for Oslo.

	At about 5 we arrived at Laerdal. There is a helicopter pad. We have camped right next to a marina. There is no ping pong. There are lots of silverburch. I am wearing a trousers, fleece + socks, but it is warm in the sun. Children here have large pedal cars.
	John + Charles have been able to hire the pedal cars.
	Heavyrain in the night. Warm rain in the morning + cloud down low.
	A delicious supper of prawns from the Bergen fish market.

[Newspaper clipping page — no handwritten diary text. Page consists entirely of printed newspaper advertisement columns including: property investment ads, investment properties in East Manchester (Blackwood), financial spread trading (CFD Index), Betting Coupes horse racing "plots", property investment offers, urban planters (www.urbanplanters.co.uk), loans and commercial property ads. A number "65" appears in printed form at top-right corner, which is part of the newspaper page number, not a diary page number.]

We left the campsite + went up a winding valley with spectacular waterfalls + rickety bridges, paths + ladders for fishmen.
     The valley opened out for a fascinating old church - Borgund stave church. It smells woody + of pitch. Dark + cosy. There are photos of funerals + christenings. In 1820 people were burnt to death in a stave church, so the gov't decreed that all church doors should open outwards + the church was modified. It was also 1800s laid down that every church should have seats for 1/3 local population. So the parishioners built another church nearby, + the old church remained.
     We came back down the valley + went to shops at __Laerdal__ + the salmon museum. In the 1980's salmon developed a parasite. The only way to kill the parasite is to kill off everything in the river, using a plant mixture. The rivers take about 4 years to get life back.

captions: Borgund stave church c 11.50. lovely smell of pitch. Dark + woody inside. Old photos of christenings, weddings + funerals. Only 2? out of 1000 stave churches remain.
pages.text:
__Borgund stave church__ c 11.50.
lovely smell of pitch. Dark + woody inside.
Old photos of christenings, weddings + funerals.
     Only 2? out of 1000 stave churches remain.

The solmon museum.
The salmon migrate to the North Atlantic to grow, but always return to their own rivers to spawn – N. America, Canada, Portugal, Russia, GB, Norway.
Temperature is important, + gravel beds. The Thames + Rhine used to be important salmon rivers.
	We went on a small ferry across the laerdal arm of Sognafjord. Through a tunnel to __Solvorngolden__ hot water unreliable. The boys are thrilled with the campsite. It's an evangelical hotel, with old fogeys (Wm's name for them) singing hymns in a chapel. A portrait of Luther. Ping pong, army slidey thing on a pulley from pine trees, + a large lake. Lots of Germans. The washing machine is a brute. Fell off its side. Simon + I were still ~~singing~~ spinning clothes at nearly midnight.
	__Sat August__
	Rain has held off, so I've hung the rest of the wash. The boys have the rubber dingh[i]es out + row across the lake. Lovely

lazy morning.
	In the afternoon John beat Eve at ping pong, William + Peter drifted in the rubber dinghies.
	Simon + Eve set off to walk 3 km down to the fjord to catch a ferry to see Urnes stavechurch, but just as we reached the harbour, the ferry set off. The wait of 1 hour, + early closing of the ferry service meant that we had to return to the campsite. Eve picked red currants on the way. There had been a row about ice creams.
	We are living well. We had reindeer pate for lunch. For supper we had smoked salmon to die for, salad, + red-currants on peanut butter on bread/rye bread for pudding.
	It's very good having the peanut butter to fill people up in the evening.
	Simon is finding the weather too cold.
	__Sunday Aug 2nd__
	We woke up to a blue sky + decided to stay on another day, thinking we would visit

the glacier in the morning, & the boys could [h]a[ve] play on the boats in the afternoon.
      We drove to the glacier museum, whi[ch] was very interesting. 3,000 years ago there wer[e] [?sou] no glaciers, 6,000 the glacier was very big Everyone in this valley died off during the Black Death, 134[9?], except for one little girl. ⅓ people in Norway died. It was very cold in 1700's; the snow of 1980's are making this glacier big now
      We left the museum & drove 2 miles to what we thought was the glacier. We couldn't see anyone at the glacier. At 12.40 we set off walking towards it. We didn't get back until 2.50. It was ENORMOUS & [?one] one of the most spectacular things I've ever seen. The reason we couldn't see anyone was they were too far away. We walked & slithered & slipped & climbed over practically smooth granite boulders, sloshed through frozen meltwater streams. Peter & Eve both fell & grazed elbows. Eve took to sliding, holding her blue jean dress under her bum, & [?grazed] her wedding ring.
      The glacier was very high 130 ft? it

[h]ad lovely blue colours in the crevasses & the cave which the water came out [of]. There was a hanging bridge. Peter [?we] found ice in the lake below.
      __Nigardsbreen__ Glacier.
      We were exhausted by the time we got back to the campsite, but Ste[r] pumped up the boat & he & Charles went off rowing. After supper we were looking forward to a quiet evening's reading. Charles was washing up & Eve was reading aloud when suddenly John came back yelling. He had fallen off the swing-out-over the water, & banged his head on the rocks, pebbles under water at 8.30. By 9.30 he had a bad headache & was vomiting. So we called a doctor, who asked John what he had been doing that day, & what day of the week it was, (… as if John ever knows!) & she sent him off to hospital, with Eve in an ambulance, to __Forde__, 90 kilometres away. Simon & the boys broke camp in record time & followed for 1½ hours in the van. We were very well looked after,

captions: A model of two Edwardians on their Harley Davigon at the Solomon museum
pages.text:
reached our room at 3am. In the [?a+d] ambulance John had a pill up his bum + oxygen in his nose + stopped feeling sick + was fine. We were finally let out at 1.0 pm.
__Monday 3rd August__
	Simon + the boys slept on the carpark + shopped at a supermarket.
	We caught the 4.0 ferry to __Ornes__ + reached a marvellous stave church
[Photo caption: A model of two Edwardians on their Harley Davigon at the Solomon museum]
We could climb up into the gallery + see beautifully carved capitals. Three families had private box pews, one of them still lives in the village - Bugge. Stave churches are built with very strong vertical pillars

[Glued-in ticket: URNES STAVKYRKJE / FORENINGEN TIL NORSKE FORTIDSMINNES- MERKERS BEVARING / Kr. 80,- / 027]

We found a beautiful valley to camp in, with a lake (drinking-water for the village, so no swimming) views of waterfalls. There was alot of rain in the night + the waterfalls increased in size; they haven't formed clefts in the hillside, they just pour out over the rocks.
__Skjolden__,** [?too] above the head of [?Luster] fjord

__Tues 4th [?August]__		__Borgsdalen__
	We left in a drizzle + went up a steep road - 8% for 10 km. It went on + on to a high area, Very barren + misty + mysterious; a crew of a glacier. Stupid

sheep next to the road.          [?Helgeflahen] [?Steinlakmos]
      We saw the beginnings of a stream
+ followed it down through lakes until
it had become a big river.
      At __Ringebu__ we saw a third stave
church with a red steeple. It is larger
than the others + has wall paintings +
big wooden beads coming down the [changlier?]
It was being painted with pitch + smelt
very strong.
      At this point we left the lowlands
again + have come up to a very lonely
plateau. The colours are mossy green +
also a very pale green plant, like a lichen
bush. Noone lives in the area + there
is no farming, but a few lakes for fishermen
+ bird spotters - but not many birds.
      We have come to wild camp, much
to William's disgust. Near __Tryvang__
Mosquitos. Near Koppang. We found the
mosquito nets.
      Wed 5th Aug
      Rain in the night, a cold, wet start, but

Ringebu Stave church.

the sun has come out now.
Mistakenly Simon, + Charles bought culture milk (yoghurt)
(6 litres) which we are still working our way thro
inventing yoghurt puddings. Our other problem
is that Norway doesn't sell Blue gas, +

changing to red would cost £70. So we are only using gas to make tea or coffee. Simon has started growing a beard.

    We stopped in __Korpang__ to shop, & then stopped in a lay-by for coffee, where we discovered we had a puncture. Simon & Charles changed the wheel while Peter fished.

    Road warning signs include "rekverk manglers" and "fakhit damperers." What are cars here powered on!

    I saw nice birch bark baskets & containers for sale lots of wild flowers. The old wooden houses are beautiful with wood fretwork around their balconies & eaves, painted(off) white. Barns are rust red with white windows & doors. The housewives crochet patterns [sketch of window/curtain pattern] along the tops of windows. Many of the small houses/wcs/barns have grass growing on the rooftops.

    We followed the river down into Sweden. [cr-] There were nothing but trees as far as the eye could see. At the Swedish campsite __Holjes__ ***[?½] we saw signs of vandalism for the first time in Scandinavia - a smashed

sign post board & broken lights.

    We camped in a very pretty meadow. It was a pity that there was no river. We walked to a restaurant & had a good 3 course meal - smoked salmon pork/beef & apple cake for £87 for 6 of us. Showers cost 5 crowns. It was a long walk to the restaurants past rather slummy looking houses - summer houses or year round?) Pikes' heads nailed to shed doors.

__Thu 6th August__

    We drove on through Sweden, so many many trees, & houses in the countryside which were not farms. The Swedish houses don't seem as elegant as the Norwegian ones.

    We drove through __Sysslebäck__ to __Torsby__, where we had to buy a new tyre for £110. At __Sunne__ the campsite was too crowded, so we went on to __Humbletorp__.

    We have camped near a lake. It's very pretty. After a load of washing, the clothes line pole broke. __Humbletorp__ ***

Fri 7th August Rain in the night, but a good windy day for drying. The boys

[photo: Selma Lagerlof's house at Marbacka]

[text at top right, partly obscured by gutter:] went out for a [bro?] on the lake in the 2 rubber boats. After lunch we came to the house. Mr [Lagerloff?] wrote Nils & the goose [Stores?]. we bought a book & [slice?] fruit bowl. [Alonly?] house which reminds [us?] of [Mackintosh?].

beautiful curtains, proportions & oak wood.

Then we drove on to Sunne. At Sunne we went to the supermarket & Eve found [bluegas?]. We had a good supper.

__Sat 8th Aug__
Lots more clothes washing, & we hired a big row boat for going out in the lake. We [like?]

the campsite, it is quite like [Cassianoel?].

In the evening we all met up in 2 large tents & celebrated "[strikethrough: Crayfish] Crayfish fest", singing + dancing + drinking + eating crayfish. Very friendly Swedes & Germans. Some have come for 20 years. It celebrates the start of the crayfish season, though now they are imported from China & USA. We met a nice family from Hamburg.

Drinking is a problem, you can buy weak ol beer in shops. For stronger drink you have to go to a special fair. So many Swedes have their own stills at home & drink very seriously.

__Sunday 9 9 Aug__
[Sushinelide?] drove to __Sunne__ & bought a paper. U.S. embassy bombings in Africa. Simon telephoned Sven in Oslo, whose wife is dying.

The houses might be based on building with logs. [sketch of log joint] So many have a central bit, as if to provide a place for logs to cross over [sketch of log joint] ***[?symbols]?

4 30. arrived in __Oslo__ campsite. V. pretty. John picked raspberries.

__Monday 10th Aug__
We had a wonderful day doing museums. We caught a bus & tram to the peninsular where 5 museums are, Bygdoy. We all visited the Viking Museum, then Simon & Eve went to the Folk [Museon?]

& saw [Gol] Stave Church & [barns] & someone spinning. The boys went to the Ship Museum. Then we all went to the Fram Museum & the Kon Tiki museum. They are marvellously laid out.
	We had hoped that the boys could swim at the pool in the park but it was shut. We looked at [Vigeland's] wonderful statues, an obelisk of bodies, [?not seen]. He showed us a restaurant where we had a delicious meal of catfish. John & Charles are fighting Svein Stoesen.
	Thank you for wanting to stop at any house any friends for the jolly good time we had together. I enjoyed every minute of it.  Affectionately, [Svein]

William - Kon Tiki: Heyerdahl burnt the Tigris in protest at the Red Sea War

Peter - Kon Tiki
John - [Gjoa] about fishing & getting fish
Charles - Gjoa - Amundsen's ship, outside & smaller than Fram, captain's cabin.
					[sketcher] [?$11] [?waterfall] [?leads] [?beds] [?free]
Simon - Viking, shapes of boats
Eve - Folkmuseum, peasant's cottage,
	 Viking shape of boats, Fram - Nansen's life, adventurer, ambassador, saved refugees

captions: The Gokstad Ship c 900 found near Oslo Fjord The preserved wood was steamed & boiled in alum & impregnated with linseed oil. Oak ship, ash tiller 32 oarsmen, helmsman, look out. Burial ship for tall, 60 year old, chieftain.
pages.text: The Gokstad Ship c 900 found near Oslo Fjord The preserved wood was steamed & boiled in alum & impregnated with linseed oil. Oak ship, ash tiller 32 oarsmen, helmsman, look out. Burial ship for tall, 60 year old, chieftain.

[postcard photo of Brekatedralen - Nigardsbreen]

Tues 12th August
   We packed up & drove to the centre to see the Akershus Festning – built by Hikon [?Magnusson] c/1300 & Christian IV early [1]7[th], lovely tapestries, wood carved chests, portraits.
   Resistance museum, 3 men ~~[strikethrough]~~ executed outside, one called Rasmussen.

We rushed up to Svein's house. He gave us a drink in the garden. Lovely wood panelling & applied arts. His sister is a ceramicist.
   He suggested we go to the beach for the boys to swim from an island to the west of Oslo. [?Hovedøya]? There was a raft & diving board lots of families but not crowded. V. hot & sunny. A marvelous resort so near a capital city. He walked round the island with Simon, & then with me, pointing out the views & islands & rich peoples houses. His wife is very ill. It was very good to spend time with him.
   We came back to the van & had a cup of tea with Svein before he went home.
   We drove on for 1 ¼ hours to [?Fiskum]**
near Kongsberg.
The campsite is a pretty old farm, with old barns & surrounded by orchards. A rifle range & trains.
   Wed 13 Aug
   We came into __Kongsberg__. There is an interesting museum – silver mining/mint/skiing/minerals? Simon bought a stone with a hole

bored in it as a candle holder. The old miners wore leather aprons at the back — arse leathers — to sit on while they worked. In 1881 they had a German lift where men could go up or down by stepping sideways onto the next rung — Until 16[?00], 20[?–] kg rock could be [?lifted] — 20 m. by a teenager using a bucket & winding handle. He was paid less than the trained breakers at the bottom. In 1680 they started using horse winches. 2 or 3 horses went round, hired from [illegible] local farms. Each farm supplied horses to work 1 week in a month. In 1880 they started using water power. The same water was used to work 18 machines on its way down the hill.
        Safety binding on skis started in 1950s
We saw the ski long jump records
9 m. in 1810 to 209 in 1994.
        Simon & Eve went to the Baroque church, but Simon got huffed at having to pay so we walked out. Lovely waterfall in the town, river near bridge. John found drill noises in good mock-up of mine very loud. William noted a Czech or Hung. crystal decanter, used to pay ski-book royalties to a Norwegian after the war, now worth £100,000.

headings: Charles picture of Transport  Aug 12th Tuesday
pages.text: Charles picture of Transport  Aug 12th Tuesday

headings: Charles picture of Transport Aug 12th Tuesday
pages.text:
Charles picture of Transport Aug 12th Tuesday

    We drove quite a way over very empty countryside. The trees are far enough apart for there to be moss on the ground. We stopped for lunch near an ants' nest, parking in front of someone's garage, which caused her to come up, walking her dog, to enquire when we'd be moving off.
    The countryside must be used by cross-country skiers.
    We came to __Rjukan-Vemork__ which is where the Norwegians had one of their first hydro electric power stations at the turn of the century. The workers' union exhibition showed how important communism was in the area.
    In 1940 the Germans took over the factory to increase the supply of "heavy water" for making the atomic bomb. The factory appeared impregnable, perched on a cliff above a gorge. (The poorer workers

had houses lower down the gorge. less sunlight. The sun stopped mid-October → midlach. Norwegians hid in a hut & plotted... blowing up the factory, US. bombing, & finally [?born] time bomb on the lake ferry as the Germans brought the heavy water out to Germany. This was very sad, because local villagers were also on the ferry.

    On to __Amot__    * * *  ; we had trouble finding a campsite, & kept pace with a friendly French family & trailer for several miles. There was a mega ant nest & blue berries. A lake. Very pretty. [?Nokmarkle] loo.
    We had a good all-you-can-eat buffet supper at a ski hotel.

__Thu 14th Aug__
    Lots & lots of rain in the night. Tent leaked. On past lake after lake to Honnevige    . Lunch at a lovely picnic spot, lots of flat boulders. John & Charles Swan [?put] up a washing line to [?try] to dry clothes.
    We went back 3 km to a farm, part of Setesdal Valley museum, Rygnesta. Excellent guide [on] called Ann.

[sketch of Main farm room: a table labelled 'table', a pot/cauldron suspended on a hook above a fire, a sleeping platform or shelf above, and what appears to be a figure or garment on the right side]

                        Main farm room

    1590. Some wood 1537 from another building. One room farm house. 2 double beds. Used until 1917. benches around wall. Only light - door & hole in roof. Long table for special occasions. Quern. Chest. High shelves, beams. Grass roof. 20 ft. high. Carved wooden bench. Only lived in until 1830. 2 or 4 people in a bed. Felting stool. Cow's bladder cover[ed] roof hole to let in light, propped up with stick [to let in] fire. hand cultivated since 900. Built until 1800 in [?Setsda].

Mid 1600 this type of house no longer built. Urnos style bench. Angels, men on horseback. Brudeback - bench with back. Earlier benches, earth underneath to insulate. Shelves for cheese + sour milk. Milk soured by smoke. Built - in beds on either side of cupboard.

[drawing of a decorated carved panel or bed surround]

5 months arms free, one year legs bound. Swaddling bands. Symbols important. Swastika woven in carved + painted wood. Large barrel for flour.
1830. Next-door room. Upper floor put in. Corner chimney. Upper floor. Pink, blue-green black + white.
    1750+ professional craftsmen made furniture.
    Fruit-bowl shape for beer, small cups, special occasion.
    Chimney in cow house to boil up bark + leaves to feed cow in winter. Horse only Sedhay. Unusual bed in storehouse.
Apart up the hill Smithy + sauna. In 1700 church said sauna spread syphilis, so sauna illegal. So sauna used to smoke ham + smoke material to de-flea it.

All buildings built by Emil [?Asmund] 1540.
2 summer farms. One only 2 km away cos animals weak in spring, + strengthened before upper farm.
    Spoon in store ho. for flour.
    Crisp bread made twice a year.
    Saddles used only for luggage.
Storehouse - upstairs amazing fabric wall-hangings, embroidery, 1470, 1550.
    We drove on + tried to dry out the tent during tea while picking raspberries. We climbed onto a plateau [?Urdwasscheii], where John needed to pee. He + Peter got out + friendly sheep came up to be stroked, making the boys nervous. There are isolated cabins here, no electricity. People park + row across the lake to their cabins. Damas. In Sept 40,000 sheep are herded down to Sirdal. Sheep pens.
    Down to Haugen to camp. Sirdal valley. excellent facilities - loos + showers v. well laid out + good quality. We had supper in the cafe. Damp but no rain.

__Friday 15th August__
    The 4 boys were dreadfully embarrassed at supper when Simon asked the man to change the dreadful modern pop music on the radio to something classical. He didn't.
    Today there are sheep lying right on the

road + we are having to thread our way between them. Yesterday's road, from over [?Orduasshei], is brand new, also the main road from Oslo → Stavanger, single track with passing places. It cut 100 km off the old journey.
      Lovely smooth rocks + cliffs + erratic boulders.
Green wires on Telegraph poles

[photo caption:] [?Stokdonmuset]. 1590's Farm Buildings.

We drove into Stavanger + saw the cathedral. Lovely C12. Heavy. some plain arches, some with dogtooth. Musicians rehearsing, wind sextet, so we could not see the cathedral fully. Marvellous 1660 paintings of families on wood panels.  ○
      A long wait on the harbour side for the ferry.
Sat 16th August
      As we left the harbour at North Shields we saw the tubes around a building of swimming slides. So we went for a super swim with lots of slides – 5 or 6 of them.
      Then we had lunch in a beautiful field where they'd harvested. Green + gold landscape. Blue sky. Not a mountain in sight. We are going south to Lincolnshire to James Smith's house to pick up Thomas who has come back in the train from visiting the Wegners.
      Thomas + Ernest Smith met us at the cross roads – in a gorgeous red Morgan. Eve got in for the 2 mile
